Broker: BytesInPerSec (input throughput), BytesOutPerSec (output throughput), UnderReplicatedPartitions (lagging replicas). JMX configuration: java -Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote ... bin/kafka-producer-perf-test.sh Example: Producer metrics retrieval: KafkaProducer<String, String> producer = new KafkaProducer<>(props); List<MetricName> metricNames = producer.metrics().keySet(); for (MetricName metric : metricNames) { double value = producer.metrics().get(metric).value(); System.out.println(metric.name() + " = " + value); } Consumer metrics: KafkaConsumer<String, String> consumer = new KafkaConsumer<>(props); Map<MetricName, ? extends Metric> metrics = consumer.metrics(); for (MetricName name : metrics.keySet()) { System.out.println(name.name() + " = " + metrics.get(name).value()); } Monitoring tools: Confluent Control Center (enterprise), Prometheus + Grafana (open source), Datadog/New Relic (cloud). Alert thresholds: record-send-rate < expected = producer slowdown. records-lag-total > 10000 = consumer lag alert. UnderReplicatedPartitions > 0 = replication issue. BytesInPerSec spike = traffic surge or attack. Custom metrics: Micrometer integration: MeterRegistry registry = new SimpleMeterRegistry(); Timer timer = Timer.builder("processing-time").register(registry); timer.record(() -> processMessage()); Metric export: Prometheus endpoint exposes metrics. Grafana dashboards visualize trends. Alert rules trigger on thresholds. Best practice: Monitor lag, replication, throughput, latency continuously. Set baseline expectations. Alert deviations early.

question: How does Kafka handle backpressure and flow control?
answer: Kafka handles backpressure when producers overwhelm brokers or consumers lag behind. Mechanisms: Producer buffering, consumer fetching limits, broker capacity. Producer-side backpressure: buffer.memory (default 32MB) accumulates unsent records. When full, producer blocks on send(). Example: Properties props = new Properties(); props.put(ProducerConfig.BUFFER_MEMORY_CONFIG, 67108864); // 64MB props.put(ProducerConfig.MAX_BLOCK_MS_CONFIG, 60000); // 60s timeout KafkaProducer<String, String> producer = new KafkaProducer<>(props); try { producer.send(record); // Blocks if buffer full } catch (BufferExhaustedException e) {} max.block.ms controls how long producer waits before throwing exception. Prevents indefinite hangs. Consumer-side flow control: fetch.min.bytes and fetch.max.wait.ms limit request frequency. max.poll.records limits records per poll. Prevents overwhelming consumer. Example: props.put(ConsumerConfig.FETCH_MIN_BYTES_CONFIG, 1024); props.put(ConsumerConfig.FETCH_MAX_WAIT_MS_CONFIG, 500); props.put(ConsumerConfig.MAX_POLL_RECORDS_CONFIG, 500); Broker-side queues: num.replica.fetchers (default 4) limits concurrent replication requests. num.network.threads limits request processing threads. Num.io.threads limits I/O operations. Backpressure scenarios: 1. Producer faster than broker: buffer accumulates, producer blocks. Indicates broker capacity issue. 2. Consumer slower than producer: lag grows. Broker throttles fetch (fetch.max.bytes). 3. Replication lag: followers slow to replicate, ISR shrinks, writes block. Handling backpressure: 1. Reduce producer rate: ramp up gradually during traffic spikes. 2. Increase consumer parallelism: add consumers. 3. Scale brokers: add nodes, increase CPU/disk. 4. Optimize processing: reduce processing latency. Monitoring: MeterRegistry tracks blocking metrics. Alert on producer blocks (buffer exhaustion). Track consumer lag. Prefer handling backpressure in application code (retry logic, circuit breaker) vs forcing blocking.

question: What are the best practices for Kafka in production?
answer: Production best practices ensure reliability, performance, and maintainability. Configuration: 1. Replication Factor: RF >= 3 for durability. Survives 2 broker failures. 2. min.insync.replicas: >= 2 for safety. Prevents silent data loss. 3. acks=all for critical data (financial), acks=1 for logs. 4. Enable idempotence: enable.idempotence=true (free deduplication). Consumer configuration: 1. Manual offset commits: disable auto-commit, commit after processing. 2. Isolation level: read_committed for consistency. 3. Consumer group per service: avoid interference. 4. Graceful shutdown: call consumer.close(). Deployment: 1. Cluster size: >= 3 brokers for HA. 2. Disk space: monitor continuously, alert at 80% capacity. 3. Network: dedicated brokers, adequate bandwidth. 4. JVM tuning: -Xmx, -Xms equal (no GC pauses from expansion), appropriate GC algorithm. Monitoring: 1. Consumer lag: alert if > 10 minutes behind. 2. ISR shrinkage: alert immediately (replication issue). 3. Unclean leader election: monitor for data loss. 4. Disk usage: alert at 80%, 90%. 5. Rebalance frequency: alert if > 1/hour. Schema management: 1. Use Schema Registry for all producers/consumers. 2. Enforce schema compatibility (FULL). 3. Version schemas, enable evolution. Data governance: 1. Retention policies: balance storage vs recovery. 2. Compacted topics: for reference data. 3. Encryption: TLS for transit, encryption at rest if required. 4. Authentication: Kerberos or SASL. Testing: 1. Chaos engineering: simulate broker failures, network partitions. 2. Performance testing: measure throughput/latency under load. 3. Integration testing: end-to-end workflows with Schema Registry. Documentation: 1. Topic purposes and retention. 2. Consumer group responsibilities. 3. Failure runbooks. 4. Performance tuning history. Compliance: GDPR/HIPAA considerations (encryption, retention, audit logs). Exactly-once: enable only for critical workflows (30-40% latency overhead).

question: What is Kafka cluster metadata and how does ZooKeeper manage it?
answer: Kafka cluster metadata includes broker registrations, topic partitions, leadership assignments, and ISR states. ZooKeeper (Kafka versions < 3.3) stores metadata in hierarchical namespace. Metadata paths: /brokers/ids (broker registrations), /brokers/topics (topic-partition assignments), /isr_change_notification (ISR changes), /controller (current controller ID). Example ZooKeeper paths: /brokers/ids/1 = broker 1 registration /brokers/topics/orders/partitions/0/state = partition leader/ISR /config/topics/orders = topic configuration ZooKeeper role: Leader election: When broker fails, ZooKeeper triggers new leader election for affected partitions. Controller selection: Single broker elected as controller (handles cluster-wide tasks). Configuration management: Topic configs, broker configs stored. ISR management: Tracks in-sync replica state, triggers ISR changes. Metadata propagation: Changes propagated to all brokers via watches. ZooKeeper watch: Brokers watch metadata paths. Changes trigger immediate notification (not polling). Example: topic partition 0 leader fails. ZooKeeper triggers election. New leader elected. Brokers notified of change. Limitations: ZooKeeper scalability limits (5-7 brokers typical). Metadata inconsistency risk (temporary). Dependency on external system. Kafka 3.3+ (KRaft): Kraft (Kafka Raft) replaces ZooKeeper. Kafka cluster becomes self-managed using Raft consensus. Advantages: Single source of truth, no external dependency, faster failover, metadata replication built-in. Migration: ZooKeeper -> KRaft is gradual (dual-write phase). Monitoring ZooKeeper: Ensure quorum healthy (odd number nodes). Monitor latency. Check disk usage. Alerts: ZooKeeper down = no cluster operations. High latency = slow failover. Configuration: zookeeper.connect=zk1:2181,zk2:2181,zk3:2181 (odd count essential).

question: Explain Kafka Quotas and throttling mechanisms.
answer: Kafka Quotas limit resource usage per client (producer/consumer), preventing resource hogging. Types: Producer quota (bytes/sec), Consumer quota (bytes/sec), Request quota (request/sec). Configuration: Per-client: quota=<limit>. Example: quota=10485760 = 10MB/sec. Broker config: quotas.window.num=11 (quota calculation windows). Example: producer quota 10MB/sec limits max output to 10MB per second. Exceeding triggers throttling (delays responses). Code example: kafka-configs --entity-type clients --entity-name client-id-1 --alter --add-config 'producer_byte_rate=10485760' kafka-configs --entity-type clients --entity-name client-id-1 --alter --add-config 'consumer_byte_rate=10485760' Monitoring: Metrics: throttle_time_ms (throttle delay). Alert if > 0 (quota exceeded). Logging: log message per throttle (configurable). Default quota: client.quota.callback.class config defines dynamic quota logic. Static quota: quotas set in broker configs. Throttling mechanics: When quota exceeded: broker delays response by throttle_time_ms. Response includes throttle duration. Client backs off accordingly (no retry storm). Per-client quotas: clients.quota.configs tracks per-client limits. Different clients isolated. Prevents single client impacting others. Example: Consumer A quota 5MB/sec, Consumer B quota 15MB/sec. B cannot starve A. Default quota: default.producer.quota, default.consumer.quota for unnamed clients. Alternatives: Use separate consumer groups for different workloads. More granular control than quotas alone. Combination: Quotas + consumer groups = fine-grained resource management. Production use: limit batch producers (prevent spikes). Protect critical consumers (ensure SLA). Prevent DoS (quota enforces fairness). Quota impact: If tight, causes throttling, increases consumer lag. Balance throughput vs fairness.

question: What is the Partition Assignment Strategy and how does it work?
answer: Partition Assignment Strategy determines how partitions distribute among consumer group members during rebalancing. Built-in strategies: RangeAssignor, RoundRobinAssignor, StickyAssignor, CooperativeStickyAssignor. RangeAssignor (default): Assigns partitions by range sequentially. Example: 10 partitions, 2 consumers. Consumer1=[0-4], Consumer2=[5-9]. Simple, but uneven when consumers vary. Code: props.put(ConsumerConfig.PARTITION_ASSIGNMENT_STRATEGY_CONFIG, "org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.RangeAssignor"); RoundRobinAssignor: Circular distribution. 10 partitions, 3 consumers. Consumer1=[0,3,6,9], Consumer2=[1,4,7], Consumer3=[2,5,8]. More even load. Code: props.put(ConsumerConfig.PARTITION_ASSIGNMENT_STRATEGY_CONFIG, "org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.RoundRobinAssignor"); StickyAssignor: Maintains sticky allocation, minimizing movement. First assignment balanced, subsequent rebalances preserve existing assignments. Example: Add Consumer3 to above. Sticky moves minimum partitions (2-3) vs Range (all). CooperativeStickyAssignor: Sticky + cooperative rebalancing (no stop-the-world). Ideal for production. Custom assignment: Implement AssignmentStrategy interface for domain-specific logic. Example: AssignmentStrategy<String, String> customStrategy = new CustomPartitionAssignor(); props.put(ConsumerConfig.PARTITION_ASSIGNMENT_STRATEGY_CONFIG, "com.example.CustomPartitionAssignor"); public class CustomPartitionAssignor implements Assignor { public GroupSubscription subscription(Set<String> topics, ConsumerMetadata metadata) { return ...; } public GroupAssignment assign(Cluster cluster, GroupSubscription subscriptions) { // Custom logic return ...; } } Assignment algorithm (RoundRobin): 1. List all partitions across subscribed topics. 2. Sort partitions and consumers. 3. Assign sequentially in round-robin. Assignor selection: Multiple strategies can configure: partition.assignment.strategy=RoundRobinAssignor,RangeAssignor. Falls back to next if first unavailable. Monitoring: kafka-consumer-groups --group my-group --describe shows current assignment. Rebalance logs show which strategy used.
